objections.objection: The implementation timeline and sequencing (Parliament amend Nkt. year 1; Ministry legislate anti-sorting rules year 1-2; teacher professionalization year 2-10; conversion year 3-9) is infeasible given documented teacher-labour constraints and concurrent reforms. The scenario assumes the existing teacher workforce can be retrained and reallocated within a 2–10 year window despite: (a) half of teachers being aged 50+ and only 7.2% under 30 (workforce replacement is slow); (b) an ongoing, capacity-consuming 2024–2031 multi-year salary reform that will occupy administrative and fiscal bandwidth; and (c) the scenario omits any modeled supply-side estimate of how many new or re-trained teachers 5-year Master's-level professionalization would require or where they would be trained. Concretely, the proposed start-of-professionalization in year 2 is unsupported by evidence that teacher-education institutions can scale acceptances or that attrition/retirement timing yields the needed reallocation without large staffing gaps or reduced instructional time. (speculative)
objections.suggested_revision: Make teacher workforce feasibility an explicit precondition: commission and publish a teacher-workforce capacity study (within 6 months) that models retirements, required retraining throughput (Masters intake), geographic distribution of needs, and overlap with the 2024–2031 salary reform. Revise the legislative timetable to a phased, pilot-led approach: (1) pilot delayed-tracking + anti-sorting rules in 2–4 districts only after workforce modelling confirms local staffing sufficiency; (2) defer national roll-out of teacher-professionalization scaling until salary-reform bandwidth releases (post-2031) or until teacher-education institutions commit to expanded intake with financed seats; (3) include explicit, funded teacher-redeployment and temporary-supply plans (substitute pools, capped class-size changes) to avoid service interruptions during conversion.

objections.objection: The plan to use an EMMI decree (year 1) and Oktatási Hivatal pilots (year 1-2) to impose SES-aware proxies and uniform thresholds assumes decree-level authority will bind state, church and foundation maintainers. This is a concrete legal and enforcement gap: church/foundation maintainers are constitutionally and contractually protected, and the scenario offers no legal mechanism showing a ministerial decree can compel their compliance. The proposed enforcement route via Kormányhivatal monitoring (year 2-4) similarly lacks demonstrated statutory authority to sanction non-state maintainers. The scenario therefore overstates near-term coverage of the reform and underestimates the risk that displaced advantaged demand will relocate immediately to non-state schools.
objections.suggested_revision: Before committing to a decree-first pathway, obtain and publish a targeted legal opinion clarifying (a) the minister's statutory reach over admission rules for non-state maintainers and (b) enforcement powers of Kormányhivatal vis-à-vis church/foundation schools. If the legal opinion finds decree-only reach insufficient, revise implementation to either: (i) build a binding incentive package (earmarked normative funding conditionality or voluntary MOUs) negotiated with church/foundation maintainers and piloted with signed agreements before nationwide rollout; or (ii) prepare a parallel Nkt. amendment roadmap with explicit timelines and cross-party engagement, acknowledging the longer lead-time and staging admission-decree changes as interim state-only pilots with mitigations for displacement.

objections.objection: The Klebelsberg Centre-led fast roll-out of minimum-score thresholds/capacity caps (timeline: year 1-2) and the Ministry's design of regulatory/fiscal levers to discourage non-state expansion (year 2-4) understate administrative and legal lead-time costs. Concretely: (a) the Klebelsberg Centre's authority to unilaterally impose new caps across maintainers is not demonstrated and would likely provoke legal challenge or require complex collective-bargaining/consultation steps that add months to years; (b) capacity caps trigger teacher-redeployment and possibly redundancies that require negotiated HR processes and budgeted redeployment funds which the scenario does not quantify; (c) the proposed fiscal levers aimed at non-state maintainers rely on contentious legal interpretations of funding agreements and could be litigated, delaying effects well beyond the stated 1–4 year window.
objections.suggested_revision: Recast the threshold/cap rollout as a staged, legally-scrutinised process: (1) commission an immediate legal authority review to map Klebelsberg's statutory powers and likely litigation risks; (2) pilot capacity caps in a small number of tankerület regions with predefined HR redeployment packages and funded transition supports (at least 12–24 months of lead funding per pilot school) to surface implementation barriers; (3) design fiscal levers only after a published legal strategy that identifies permissible incentive mechanisms (e.g., voluntary top-up grants, matching funds withheld unless expansion is documented) and secure a contingency litigations budget/timeline; (4) extend national timelines to 3–6 years to accommodate consultations, legal clearance, and funded teacher-redeployment.

objections.objection: The proposal to design and statutorily protect a TEIP-style targeted funding formula within year 1-2 underestimates Hungary's fiscal and administrative constraints. Specifically: (a) the fiscal context (projected 6.2% deficit) makes rapid cardinal-law protection politically unlikely and underestimates the political lead-time for cross-party agreement; (b) rolling out targeted funding at scale requires new administrative capacity for needs-assessment, disbursement monitoring and outcome evaluation that the scenario does not budget or schedule; (c) the scenario assumes durability of targeted lines without specifying automatic indexation or legally enforceable triggers to survive consolidation cycles, leaving the funding vulnerable to early cuts.
objections.suggested_revision: Strengthen the funding-protection and administrative plan before full roll-out: (1) produce a published multi-year fiscal costing and a politically realistic financing pathway (phased increases, reallocation sources, or EU/co-financing options) within 6 months; (2) pilot TEIP-style funding in a limited set of districts with built-in independent evaluation and a small centralised monitoring unit to demonstrate effect and administrative workflows before scaling; (3) seek a medium-term cross-party budget commitment instrument (timelimited statute or multi-year expenditure framework) combined with automatic protection features (e.g., indexed floor tied to pupil-weighted formula) to reduce vulnerability to short-term consolidation cuts; (4) explicitly budget and resource the local administrative capacity-building (training, IT, reporting templates) required to avoid implementation failure.
